title: CHAPTER 12 —
CAPSTONE: A CLAUDE-SUPPORTED UNIT
segments.body_html: Capability built: Design a complete unit or module using Claude — from learning outcomes through assessment — with human

beats.text: Capability built: Design a complete unit or module using Claude ,  from learning outcomes through assessment ,  with human review gates at every stage and a verification checklist that confirms the teacher, not the tool, made the decisions that matter.

segments.label: 01 / 13 · HOOK

title: WHAT THIS
CHAPTER PRODUCES.
segments.body_html:
Learning outcomes — What students will be able to do, not what topics will be covered
Lesson sequence — Activity-by-activity plan with backward-design logic
Materials — Readings, prompts, handouts, and examples — adapted for your learners
Feedback plan — When, how, and from whom students receive feedback, and what they must do with it

beats.text: The capstone deliverable has seven components. Each was introduced in an earlier chapter; this is the first time they appear together, connected by a single teaching purpose. These seven components are not independent. They must cohere: the assessment must measure the outcomes; the feedback plan must serve the assessment; the materials must build toward the lessons; the AI-use policy must be consistent with the assessment design. Claude can help draft each component. The coherence check is your job.

segments.label: 02 / 13 · WHAT THIS CHAPTER PRODUCES

title: STEP 1:
DRAFT THE LEARNING OUTCOMES
segments.body_html: The backward-design principle (Wiggins and McTighe, 2005):→Claude prompt — outcomes draft:→Claude output (condensed):→Human review decision:

beats.text: The backward-design principle (Wiggins and McTighe, 2005): Begin with what you want students to be able to do, then design activities and assessments that produce and reveal that ability. Do not start from activities. I am designing a three-week argument writing unit for a first-year general education writing course. Students have varied prior writing experience. At the end of this unit, I want students to be able to write a clearly argued, evidence-supported essay on a topic they have researched.

segments.label: 03 / 13 · STEP 1: DRAFT THE LEARNING OUT

title: STEP 2:
DESIGN THE LESSON SEQUENCE
segments.body_html: Claude prompt — lesson sequence:→Claude output (condensed — three representative meetings):→Meeting 1 — What Is an Argument?→Meeting 4 — Source Evaluation→Meeting 7 — Counterargument Workshop

beats.text: I have five learning outcomes for a three-week argument writing unit. I will paste them below. Please design a lesson-by-lesson sequence (nine class meetings, 75 minutes each) using backward design logic: activities should build toward the outcomes, and each meeting should do assessable cognitive work, not just deliver content. Include what students do before, during, and after each class. Flag any meeting where the cognitive work is lower than I probably want. Claude output (condensed ,  three representative meetings):

segments.label: 04 / 13 · STEP 2: DESIGN THE LESSON SEQU

title: STEP 3:
GENERATE MATERIALS
segments.body_html: One material needed:→Claude prompt — checklist generation:→Claude output:→Human review decision:

beats.text: One material needed: The source evaluation checklist mentioned in Meeting 4. Claude prompt ,  checklist generation:

segments.label: 05 / 13 · STEP 3: GENERATE MATERIALS

title: STEP 4:
BUILD THE DIFFERENTIATION PLAN
segments.body_html: CAST UDL guidelines (CAST, 2018)→Claude prompt — differentiation review:→Claude output (condensed):→Human review decision:

beats.text: CAST UDL guidelines (CAST, 2018) frame differentiation as offering multiple means of representation, action and expression, and engagement ,  not as creating separate tracks for different students. Claude prompt ,  differentiation review:

segments.label: 06 / 13 · STEP 4: BUILD THE DIFFERENTIAT

title: STEP 5:
WRITE THE FEEDBACK PLAN
segments.body_html: Claude prompt — feedback plan:→Claude output:→Flag from Claude:→Human review decision:

beats.text: A feedback plan answers four questions: When does feedback happen? What form does it take? Who gives it? What must the student do with it (Brookhart, 2017)? For this argument writing unit, please draft a feedback plan that answers: When does feedback happen in the sequence? What form does it take (written, verbal, peer, instructor)? Who gives it? What is the student's required response to feedback? The plan should ensure that feedback happens early enough to improve the final essay ,  not just on the final draft.

segments.label: 07 / 13 · STEP 5: WRITE THE FEEDBACK PLA

title: STEP 6:
DESIGN THE ASSESSMENT
segments.body_html: Claude prompt — assessment design:→Claude output (condensed):→Human review decision:

beats.text: The assessment must measure whether students achieved the learning outcomes. It must not be an artifact test ,  a test of whether they produced a document ,  but a learning test (Ambrose et al., 2010). I have five learning outcomes for my argument writing unit. Please design an assessment that measures each outcome directly. The final essay cannot be the only evidence ,  at least some outcomes must be measured through process evidence, not just product.

segments.label: 08 / 13 · STEP 6: DESIGN THE ASSESSMENT

title: STEP 7:
WRITE THE STUDENT AI-USE POLICY
segments.body_html: Claude prompt — AI-use policy draft:→Claude output (condensed):→AI-Use Policy — Argument Writing Unit→Human review decision:

beats.text: The policy must be teachable ,  specific enough that students know what is and is not allowed, honest about the reasoning, and consistent with the assessment design (Chapter 10). For this argument writing unit, please draft a student AI-use policy that: (1) specifies which parts of the unit students may use AI tools to assist with; (2) specifies which parts they may not; (3) explains the reasoning in plain language; (4) states the disclosure requirement; (5) is consistent with the assessment design.

segments.label: 09 / 13 · STEP 7: WRITE THE STUDENT AI-U

title: THE VERIFICATION
CHECKLIST.
segments.body_html:
·Outcomes specify what students will do, not what will be covered
·Each outcome is assessable by at least one piece of evidence in the assessment plan
·Outcomes were written or reviewed by the instructor, not accepted verbatim from Claude
·Each meeting requires active student cognitive work
·The sequence builds toward the outcomes (backward design logic verified)

beats.text: Before the unit goes live, you run it through a final check. This is the record of the unit's integrity ,  where Claude helped and where human judgment decided. Outcomes Outcomes specify what students will do, not what will be covered Each outcome is assessable by at least one piece of evidence in the assessment plan Outcomes were written or reviewed by the instructor, not accepted verbatim from Claude

segments.label: 10 / 13 · THE VERIFICATION CHECKLIST

title: THE AI WAYBACK MACHINE:
WHERE THIS PRACTICE COMES FROM
segments.body_html: Grant Wiggins and Jay McTighe→CAST→Susan Brookhart

beats.text: Grant Wiggins and Jay McTighe published Understanding by Design in 1998 (Wiggins and McTighe, 2005). Their argument was simple and durable: start with what you want students to be able to do, then design backward to activities and assessments. A quarter-century later, that logic is exactly what prevents AI-assisted unit design from becoming artifact assembly. The backward design discipline is not about Claude; it is about whether the unit is actually designed for learning.

segments.label: 11 / 13 · THE AI WAYBACK MACHINE: WHERE
